Threat.value: Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively
Having strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame

Negative Appraisals.value: I used to be a happy person but now I am always miserable.
I have to be especially careful because you never know what can happen next
I will never be able to feel normal emotions again

Memory.value: When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.
I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.

Maladaptive Strategies.value: try hard not think about the trauma
I try to erase the memory of the event

vignette: As a 73-year-old non-binary individual from an African background, this patient's life has been forever altered by a traumatic experience of violence. The sound of motorcycles still sends shivers down their spine, instantly transporting them back to that fateful day. The auditory triggers are relentless, and even the slightest stress can set off a chain reaction of irritability, angry outbursts, and difficulty sleeping. The patient's world has shrunk significantly since the trauma. They are perpetually on edge, always scanning their surroundings for potential threats. This hyper-vigilance has become exhausting, leaving them feeling like they're walking on eggshells, never knowing what might happen next. Their emotional landscape has also shifted dramatically; they often find themselves overcome with strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, and shame. Memories of the traumatic event continue to haunt the patient. They frequently recall the sounds they heard during the incident, which can be so vivid that it feels like they're reliving the moment all over again. The memories are seared into their mind, yet somehow, they struggle to put them into words. This disconnection from their emotions has left them feeling numb and disconnected from the world around them. In an attempt to cope with these overwhelming emotions and memories, the patient has developed a few maladaptive strategies. They avoid wearing clothes that remind them of the traumatic event, try to suppress thoughts about what happened, and even attempt to erase the memory altogether. These coping mechanisms offer fleeting relief but ultimately perpetuate the cycle of distress. The cumulative effect of these symptoms has taken a devastating toll on the patient's relationships and overall well-being. They feel like they've lost their sense of happiness and are now stuck in a state of perpetual misery. Their relationships have become strained, as loved ones struggle to understand why this once vibrant individual is now so withdrawn and irritable. The patient's world has been reduced to a constant struggle for survival, with the trauma lingering in every corner, waiting to pounce.
